attributes.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a three month history of pain in his right calf when he walks more than 100 metres. The pain ceases when he rests. He has no other symptoms and his medical history is unremarkable. He is overweight and has a 30 pack-year smoking history. On examination his blood pressure is 150/100 mm Hg and his pulse rate is 90/min. Both lower limbs and feet are warm and well perfused. The right femoral pulse is diminished in volume and the right pedal pulse cannot be felt. The ankle-brachial index is 0.9 on the left and 0.7 on the right. There are no other abnormal physical findings. He is commenced on aspirin and a statin in addition to receiving smoking cessation counselling.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
The patient's symptoms are indicative of intermittent claudication, an ischaemic pain in the limb as a result of poor oxygenated blood flow to the tissues. The reduced ankle-branchial index (<0.9) confirms that the patient's symptoms are due to peripheral artery disease. The reduction of palpable pulses is also indicative the a peripheral vascular disease diagnosis clinically. The presence of Peripheral Vascular Disease is associated with a significantly reduced 5 year survival due to increased cardiac and cerebral mortality.

His disease severity might be classified as mild. In the first instance there should be a focus on risk modification and in his case, this will involve addressing his tobacco use and considering the use of a statin and aspirin (both reduce risk of cardiac or cerebral events). This should be completed whilst also referring the man to an allied health professional for a supervised exercise program. There is volumes of evidence that exercise programs significantly improve walking distance and reduces claudication in patients with peripheral vascular disease. This lifestyle modification should be implemented before or with pharmaceutical intervention such as antiplatelet therapy and statin therapy.

Cilostazol is a symptom relief medication used in peripheral vascular disease. It is a phosphodiesterase inhibitor that suppresses platelet aggregation and is a direct arterial vasodilator. This is not a first line management option for this condition, but should be considered in the near future should a supervised exercise program, statin therapy and antiplatelet therapy be unsuccessful and controlling the man's symptoms.

Propranolol is a beta blocker medication that is not indicated in the direct treatment of peripheral vascular disease. They may be beneficial in reducing cardiovascular risk factors in patients with coronary artery disease, angina and/or congestive heart failure.

Amlodipine is a calcium channel blocker that could be used to manage the patient's hypertension. While blood pressure control is important in peripheral vascular disease management, it is not the most appropriate initial management for the claudication symptoms. The focus should be on lifestyle modification with a supervised exercise program first.

Isosorbide mononitrate is a nitrate vasodilator that is primarily used for angina pectoris. While it causes vasodilation, it is not an established treatment for peripheral arterial disease or intermittent claudication. The vasodilation it produces is more pronounced in the venous system and coronary arteries rather than peripheral arteries. Additionally, nitrates can cause significant side effects including headaches and hypotension, and there is no evidence supporting their use in the management of intermittent claudication.
learningPoints: Peripheral vascular disease management should begin with lifestyle modification before or with initial pharmaceutical management
references: Bich Au T et al. Peripheral arterial disease. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2013;42:397-400. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/june/peripheral-arterial-disease-diagnosis
Ref: Kullo IJ, Rooke TW. Peripheral artery disease. N Engl J Med 2016; 374:861-71.

Lane R, Harwood A, Watson L, Leng GC. Exercise for intermittent claudication. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017 Dec 26;12(12):CD000990. doi: 10.1002/14651858.CD000990.pub4. PMID: 29278423; PMCID: PMC6486315.
Reilly MP, Mohler ER 3rd. Cilostazol: treatment of intermittent claudication. Ann Pharmacother. 2001 Jan;35(1):48-56. doi: 10.1345/aph.19408. PMID: 11197586.
Neschis DG, Golden MA, Eid J. Clinical features and diagnosis of lower extremity peripheral artery disease. UpToDate. Waltham: UpToDate. 2018.
